1.description: Nonlinear expected returns. This figure shows the relationship between the six-month cumulative equity market return (CRSP value-weighted U.S. equity market portfolio) and the six-month lag of the VIX in red, as well as the relationship between the six-month cumulative one-year Treasury return (CRSP one-year constant maturity Treasury portfolio) and the six-month lag of the VIX in blue. Both nonlinear relationships are estimated using sieve reduced-rank regressions on a cross-section of stocks and bonds. They-axis is expressed as a ratio of returns to the full-sample standard deviation. Thex-axis shows the VIX. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

2.description: p-values by forecast horizon: 1990 to 2014. This figure plotsp-values by forecast horizon for linear and polynomial VIX predictive regressions. The regressionsRxt+hi=a0i+a1i(vixt)+εt+hiandRxt+hi=b0i+b1i(vixt)+b2i(vixt)2+b3i(vixt)3+εt+hiare estimated by OLS forh=1,…24, whereiranges over 1-, 2-, 3-, 5-, and 10-year Treasury excess returns and stock market excess returns.p-values for Wald tests of joint significance of slope coefficientsH0:b1i=b2i=b3i=0using Hodrick (1992) standard errors are reported. The sample period is 1990:1 to 2014:9. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

3.description: p-values by forecast horizon: 1990 to 2007. This figure plotsp-values by forecast horizon for linear and polynomial VIX predictive regressions. The regressionsRxt+hi=a0i+a1i(vixt)+εt+hiandRxt+hi=b0i+b1i(vixt)+b2i(vixt)2+b3i(vixt)3+εt+hiare estimated by OLS forh=1,…24, whereiranges over 1-, 2-, 3-, 5-, and 10-year Treasury excess returns and stock market excess returns.p-Values for Wald tests of joint significance of slope coefficientsH0:b1i=b2i=b3i=0using Hodrick (1992) standard errors are reported. The sample period is 1990:1 to 2007:7. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

4.description: Univariate nonparametric and polynomial estimates ofϕhi(v). This figure shows sieve, polynomial, and kernel estimates of the nonlinear volatility functionϕhi(v)from univariate predictive regressionsRxt+hi=ϕhi(vt)+εt+h, whereEt[Rxt+hi]=ϕhi(vt). The superscriptiindexes regressions in which the left-hand-side variable is either equity market excess returns (solid line) or one-year Treasury excess returns (dashed line). In Panel A, the sample consists of monthly observations onvt=VIXtfrom 1990:1 to 2014:9, whereas in Panel B, the sample consists of monthly observations from 1990:1 to 2007:7. In both panels, the forecast horizon plotted ish=6months. Within each panel, the left graph shows the nonparametric sieve estimate ofϕhi(vt), where the number of B-spline basis functions used in the estimation is chosen by out-of-sample cross-validation. The middle graph shows a parametric cubic polynomial regression whereϕhi(vt)=a0i+a1ivt+a2ivt2+a3ivt3. The right graph showsϕhi(vt)estimated by a Nadaraya-Watson kernel regression, where the bandwidth is chosen by Silverman's rule of thumb. They-axis is rescaled by the unconditional standard deviation ofRxtito display risk-adjusted returns. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

5.description: Cross-sectional pricing. This figure plots results of the unrestricted joint forecasting regressionsRxt+1i=ai+biϕ(vt)+εt+1iagainst the restricted joint forecasting regressionsRxt+1i=(αi+βiλ0)+βiλ1ϕ(vt)+βiut+1+εt+1iobtained from a dynamic asset pricing model with affine prices of risk. The innovationsut+1=Yt+1−Et[Yt+1]correspond to the cross-sectional pricing factorsYt=(MKTt,TSY1t,ϕ(vt)), whereMKTis the return to the CRSP value-weighted equity market return,TSY1 is the return to the one-year Treasury, andϕ(vt)is the nonlinear pricing factor forvt=vixt. Excess returns overicorrespond to 11 equity portfolios sorted by industry from Ken French's website, seven maturity-sorted Treasury portfolios, the six Barclay's industry- and ratings-sorted corporate bond portfolios, and the CRSP market return. To obtain estimates, the unrestricted regression is estimated by sieve reduced-rank regression, yielding parametric estimates ofaiandbiand a nonparametric estimate ofϕ(vt). The restricted joint forecasting regression is then estimated by takingϕ(vt)as given, makingaiandbifrom the unrestricted forecasts directly comparable to(αi+βiλ0)andβiλ1in the restricted regressions. The comparisons are scattered in the plots. The sample consists of monthly observations from 1990:1 to 2014:9. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

6.description: Comparison to other variables. This figure plots a nonlinear function of the VIX,ϕ6(vt), estimated by sieve reduced-rank regression against known price of risk, liquidity, and uncertainty factors: the term spread between the 10-year and three-month Treasury yield (TERM), the Cochrane-Piazzesi Factor (CP), the spread between Moody's Baa-rated corporate bonds and the 10-year Treasury yield (DEF), the log dividend yield (DY), the Lettau-Ludvigson CAY factor (CAY), the variance risk premium (VRP), the Kyle-Obizhaeva illiquidity factor (ILL), and the Jurado-Ludvigson-Ng macro uncertainty variable. To facilitate visual comparisons, all variables are demeaned and scaled by their unconditional standard deviations. The sample period is 1990:1 to 2014:9. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

7.description: Fund flows,ϕh(vixt),and flight-to-safety. Using a panel of mutual fund flows, we estimate sieve reduced rank regressionsFlowsti=ai+biϕFF(vixt)+εti. The top left graph shows the predicted fund flowsF̂lowsti=âi+b̂iϕ̂FF(vixt)for the subset of fund types that were found to load significantly onϕFF(vixt)(TableVI). The dashed vertical line indicates when government bond funds experience net inflows. The top right graph showsϕ̂FF(v)together withϕ̂h(v)and its 95% pointwise confidence interval estimated separately from sieve reduced-rank regressionsRxt+hi=ahi+bhiϕh(vt)+εt+hi, whereh=6months andi=1,…,nranges over the CRSP value-weighted market excess return and the seven CRSP constant-maturity Treasury excess returns corresponding to 1, 2, 5, 7, 10, 20, and 30 years to maturity. Given the different units in each regression,ϕFF(v)is affine-translated to the scale ofϕh(v). The sample period is 1990:1 to 2014:9. The bottom graph plots values of the VIX above its sample median (left axis, solid blue line) as well as combined stock fund outflows and government bond fund inflows (right axis, dashed red line). Stock fund outflows are the sum of U.S. equity, non-U.S. equity, and hybrid equity mutual fund outflows. The sample consists of monthly observations from 2000:1 to 2014:12. Source: ICI Trends in Mutual Fund Activity. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

8.description: SRR regression: Value-at-risk cross-section and the VIX. The top graph of this figure plots the VIX (left axis, solid line) next to major dealer banks' summed Value-at-Risks (VaR) (right axis, dashed line) over time. The bottom graph shows the estimatedϕVaR(vix)from the contemporaneous sieve reduced-rank regression of dealers' disaggregated VaRs on the VIX,VaRti=ai+biϕVaR(vixt)+εti, wherei=1,…,5indexes individual VaRs of the dealer banks that comprise the aggregate measure: Bank of America, Citigroup, Goldman Sachs, JP Morgan, and Morgan Stanley. Dotted lines represent 95% confidence intervals from the test ofH3,0in Theorem1. The sample consists of quarterly observations from 2004:1 to 2014:4. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
